coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots from wheat straw for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of fluoride and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shaohua
Liu, Zengchen
Li, Qingfeng
Xia, Hongjun
Yang, Weijie
Wang, Ruixia
Li, Yanxia
Zhao, Hui
Tian, Boshi
coredata.dc:description: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of F− have attracted enormous interest owing to their simplicity, low-cost and high selectivity. However, traditional colorimetric and fluorescent sensors mainly based on the insoluble and toxic organic molecules, which is not favorable for sensing F− in water media and living cells. In this work, we designed fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-model probe for the detection of F−. The CDs were prepared by a green, one-step hydrothermal strategy from wheat straw without any additives and surface passivation. The obtained CDs exhibited a bright blue fluorescence, special response to F− and low cytotoxicity. More interestingly, a significant color change from light yellow to red can be observed by the naked eye upon addition of F− ions to the CDs solution probably due to the formation of hydrogen bonding between CDs and F−. Besides, the fluorescence of CDs also can be selectively quenched by F− with the detection limit of about 49 μM. Additionally, the CDs are also applied to intracellular imaging and sensing of F− in living cells. This strategy may provide a new method for the detection of F− in water media and biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colorimetric/fluorescent probe
Fluoride anion
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric detection of F−
Cytotoxicity of CDs and its intracellular imaging of F−

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) hydrodynamic diameter distribution histogram, (c) XRD pattern and (d) zeta potential of CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CDs, the corresponding deconvolution spectra of (b) C1s peak and (c) O 1s peak. (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, PL excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (b) PL emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. Inset: the photographs of CDs solution under the daylight (left) and UV lamp (right, λex = 365 nm). The effect of (c) pH and (d) concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs aqueous solution.

(a) Color changes and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs solution upon addition of different concentrations of F− ions. (c) Photography of CDs solution after addition of different ions. (d) The proposed structure of CDs and mechanism for identifying F−.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra and photograph of CDs under UV light (inset) after addition of different concentrations of F− ions (0– 70 mM). (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of F− ions. (c) The linear relationship between F/F
0 and concentrations of F− from 0 to 1.5 mM. (d) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the presence of various individual ions. (e) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the absence and presence of F−. (f) Hydrodynamic diameter of CDs in the presence of F−.

(a) Cytotoxicity assay of HeLa cells in the presence of CDs with different concentrations of CDs. (b) Photography of green bean under sunlight (top) and UV light (down) after culture with water and CDs solution (1.0 mg/mL) for 5 days, respectively. (c) Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells after incubation with CDs and CDs + F−, respectively.
The schematic synthesis procedure of CDs and fluorescence/colorimetric detection of F− ion.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots from wheat straw for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of fluoride and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications; Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of F− have attracted enormous interest owing to their simplicity, low-cost and high selectivity. However, traditional colorimetric and fluorescent sensors mainly based on the insoluble and toxic organic molecules, which is not favorable for sensing F− in water media and living cells. In this work, we designed fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-model probe for the detection of F−. The CDs were prepared by a green, one-step hydrothermal strategy from wheat straw without any additives and surface passivation. The obtained CDs exhibited a bright blue fluorescence, special response to F− and low cytotoxicity. More interestingly, a significant color change from light yellow to red can be observed by the naked eye upon addition of F− ions to the CDs solution probably due to the formation of hydrogen bonding between CDs and F−. Besides, the fluorescence of CDs also can be selectively quenched by F− with the detection limit of about 49 μM. Additionally, the CDs are also applied to intracellular imaging and sensing of F− in living cells. This strategy may provide a new method for the detection of F− in water media and biological systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride plays a key role in human health and animal activities. Appropriate fluoride can be used to prevent dental caries and treat osteoporosis [1]. However, excessive intake of fluoride may cause serious skeletal fluorosis and induced liver and kidney damage. More seriously, a high level of fluoride ion may disorder the synthesis of protein and DNA, leading to the damage of the immune system and even death [2]. Therefore, developing effective methods for sensing of fluoride anion is urgently needed.

Currently, a variety of methods, such as ion-selective electrodes, ion chromatography, spectrophotometry, colorimetric and fluorometric method, have been developed for sensing F− [3–8]. Among them, colorimetric and fluorometric methods have gained extremely interest due to their advantages of simplicity, low cost, high selectivity and sensitivity [9,10]. At present, the colorimetric and fluorescent chemosensors for sensing F− mainly focus on organic receptors such as anthracene [11], azo [12], benzimidazole [13], urea [14,15], naphthalimide [16,17], Schiff base [18,19] and so on. However, almost all these receptors have some disadvantages such as high toxicity, complicated procedures or insoluble in water. Besides, the above chemosensors are not suitable for sensing F− in living cells due to their poor biocompatibility. Thus, it is essential to develop a novel chemosensor with good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility for sensing F− in living cells.

Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained more attention for optical sensing owing to their outstanding fluorescent feature, good photostability, excellent biocompatibility and facile synthesis [20–26]. Many efforts have been devoted to developing fluorescence probes based on carbon dots for sensing various metal ions such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr(VI) and Ag+ and so on [27–36]. Up to now, however, utilizing carbons dots as a probe for sensing F− is rarely reported. For instance, U. Baruah et al. designed an F− detection probe based on CDs modified with β-cyclodextrin, and they found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased gradually with the addition of F− [37]. Liu and co-workers reported a Zr(H2O)2EDTA functionalized carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for detection of F− based on the competitive ligand reactions between the CDs and F− coordinated to Zr(H2O)2EDTA [38]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report for colorimetric detection of F− based on carbon dots as receptors.

Herein, we reported fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-model probe for the detection of F− in water media (Scheme 1 ). The carbon dots can be prepared by hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw without any additives or post-synthetic surface passivation. The obtained CDs exhibited bright fluorescent emission, excellent dispersibility in water and good biocompatibility. Moreover, a significant color change from light yellow to red along with the fluorescent quenching can be observed by the naked eye upon addition of F− ions into the CDs solution. Additionally, the obtained CDs also can be utilized as a fluorescent probe for cellular imaging and detection F− in living cells. Therefore, this simple, economical technique for detection of F− will have prospective applications in the detection of F− and cell imaging.

NaF, NaCl, NaBr, KI, NaNO3, NH4F, Na2SO4 NaHCO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, triphosphate (TP), glutathione (GSH), tryptophan (Trp), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), methionine (Met) and glucose (Glc) and lysine (Lys) were obtained from Aladdin Corporation. Wheat straw was obtained from local farmland (Zhoukou City, Henan, P.R. China). All other chemicals were used as directly without any purification.

CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.1 g of dried wheat straw was cut into smaller pieces and washed with water and ethanol sequentially. Then put it into 30 mL of deionized water. The mixture was sealed in a 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h and cooled to room temperature naturally. After that, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the insoluble precipitate, and the resultant supernatant solution was collected and passed through a 0.22 μm micron filter.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by MTT assay on HeLa cells. Briefly, HeLa cells with a density of about 8000 cells per well were cultured in a 96-well plate at 37 °C overnight. Then the CDs solution with various concentrations was added in the test wells and continuous culture for another 24 h. After that, 20 μL of MTT solution (5.0 mg/mL) was added and further incubated for 4 h. At last, 150 μL of DMSO was added after removal of the supernatant and absorbance was recorded at 490 nm using a microtiter plate reader. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
Cell viability
%
=
OD treated
OD control
× 100 %
where OD treated and OD control is the optical density in the presence and absence of CDs, respectively.

For fluorescent confocal imaging, HeLa cells were cultured on coverslips in 6-well plates. Then, CDs solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. After rinsed with PBS three times, the cells were immobilized with 1 mL of 2.5% formaldehyde and imaged on confocal microscopy (Leica, SP8).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was carried out on JEOL-2100F at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on Bruker D8 Focus diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a PHI 5300 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The hydrodynamic diameter of nanoparticles was determined on Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed using a Nicolet 5700 spectrophotometer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded using a Lambder 25 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on an FLS920P fluorescence spectrophotometer. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were obtained on Leica SP8.

For F− detection, 3.0 mL of CDs solution was taken into the cuvette, and then a certain amount of F− was added to the CDs solution and incubated for 1 min. The changes in fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were recorded. The selectivity of CDs toward F− ions was also carried out by adding other ions instead of F− in the same way.

The morphology and structure of CDs were characterized by TEM and the images are shown in Fig. 1 . Clearly, the obtained CD showed good dispersibility with an average diameter of about 2.1 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter obtained by DLS was about 5.7 nm (Fig. 1b), which was larger than the diameter obtained by TEM due to the hydration of CDs in water. The HRTEM images (inset of Fig. 1a) showed that CDs possessed a well crystalline structure with a lattice space of about 0.22 nm, which is consistent with the graphitic C (100) diffraction plane [20]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) displayed a broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 22°, indicating the amorphous structure of CDs [39]. As shown in Fig. S1, the Raman spectrum of CDs displayed the characteristic band of graphitic carbon materials at D (1364 cm−1) and G band (1572 cm−1) [26], further confirming the graphitic-like structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the zeta potential was estimated to be about −25.7 mV, suggesting the negatively charged surface of CDs.

The detailed chemical composition and surface chemistry of CDs were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As displayed in Fig. 2a, the survey XPS spectrum of CDs showed two strong bands at 287.3 and 531.8 eV, corresponding to the C1s and O1s, respectively [40]. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the high-resolution spectra of C1s showed four peaks located at 284.7, 285.7, 287.6 and 289.1 eV, which could be assigned to the CC, C-OH, CO and -COOH, respectively [41]. As displayed in Fig. 2c, the O1s band was divided into two peaks at 531.4 and 533.0 eV, representing the C-O-C and CO, respectively. These results suggest that the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are present on the surface of CDs. Fig. 2d displays the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. The peaks located at 1718 and 1629 cm−1 arising from the stretching vibrations of CO and CC groups, respectively. Two absorption bands at 1096 and 3424 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C and C–OH, respectively [42]. The FT-IR results reveal that the obtained CDs indeed contain many hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which are well consistent with the XPS analysis.

Then, we further investigated the optical properties of CDs by the fluorescent spectrum and UV–vis absorption. As displayed in Fig. 3a (inset), a transparent and light-yellow solution can be observed under daylight, suggesting the excellent dispersity of CDs in water. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs showed an intensive absorption band at 278 nm (Fig. 3a), resulting from the π-π* transition of CO [43]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the emission spectrum of CDs exhibited a broadband with a maximum at 470 nm upon excitation at 370 nm. Correspondingly, a bright fluorescence emission (inset of Fig. 3a) was observed under UV light (λex = 365 nm). As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs showed broad emission with the increase of excitation wavelength from 340 to 500 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved when the excitation wavelength was 380 nm. The fluorescence emission was highly dependent on the excitation wavelengths that may be resulting from their ununiform size and surface states [24,44]. The quantum yield (QY) of the obtained CDs was calculated to be about 7.5%. (The detailed calculation process was given in Supporting Information).

Moreover，the photostability of as-prepared CDs under different conditions was further investigated. The CDs solution still displayed bright blue emission after continuous irradiation by a UV lamp for 6 h, revealing that CDs possess good anti-photobleaching (Fig. S2). Fig. 3c showed the fluorescence intensities almost remained constant in a pH ranging from 2 to 9, which is beneficial for their bio-imaging applications. In addition, the fluorescence intensity decreased mildly with the NaCl concentration up to 1.0 mg L−1 (Fig. 3d). Further, to investigate the feasibility of CDs as a fluorescent biomarker in biological systems, the impact of some intracellular compounds (triphosphate, glutathione, tryptophan leucine, isoleucine, methionine and glucose) and ions (K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and HCO3 −) was studied. It was found from Fig. S3 that no obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution were observed upon their exposure to these interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric detection of F−
ce:section.ce:para: Notably, the functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) on the CDs surface are expected to selectively detection because they can interact with specific ions. On the other hand, the small size and large charge density of fluorine make them easy to form hydrogen bond with hydrogen donors (e.g., C, O and N atom). Previous research has confirmed that many organic probes (such as azo, urea and benzothiazole) can be utilized as colorimetric probe for detection F−, and the mechanism can be ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bond between F− and receptors [1]. Inspired by previous studies, we anticipated that the obtained CDs can be used as a colorimetric probe to detect F−. As displayed in Fig. 4a, a visual color change from light yellow to red can be observed by the naked eye after CDs solution incubation with F− for 1 min, indicating that the CDs can be acted as a visual colorimetric probe for fast sensing F− ion in water media. Correspondingly, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs showed that the absorbance around 600 nm gradually increased with increasing F− concentration (Fig. 4b). As illustrated in Fig. 4c, we proposed that the color change possibly resulted from the change of electronic structure of the CDs due to the hydrogen-bonding interactions (F⋯HO) between the carboxyl/hydroxyl groups of CDs and F− ions. As shown in Fig. 4d, no obvious color change was observed for CDs solution after the addition of Cl−, Br− and I− excepted for F−, suggesting the high selective sensing toward F−. This can be ascribed to the stronger electronegativity of F− ions than other halide ions, resulting in the stronger hydrogen-bonding interactions between CDs and F− ions.

Simultaneously, we also investigated the feasibility of using the prepared CDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of F−. As depicted in Fig. 5a the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased significantly with the increase of F− ions concentrations from 5.0 to 50 mM (Fig. 5b), indicating that the F− ions can quench the fluorescence of CDs. Correspondingly, the blue fluorescence almost quenched after addition of F− (inset of Fig. 5a). This could be ascribed to the electron or energy transfer of CDs, arising from the strong affinity of F− for carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CDs, thus accelerating the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through the electron or charge transfer and causing the fluorescence quenching [45,46]. To further understanding the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence decay traces of CDs in the absence and presence of F− was investigated. As displayed in Fig. 5e, the average lifetime of CDs (9.86 ns) was closed to that of CDs + F− (9.23 ns), indicating a static quenching mechanism. In addition, the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs + F− (Fig. 5f) was estimated to be about 19.8 nm, which is larger than that of CDs in the absence of F−, possibly due to the formation of CDs aggregation arising from the interaction between F− and carboxyl group on the surface of CDs. Therefore, the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism may be due to the static quenching caused by F− induced CD aggregation.

Fig. 5c displays the normalized fluorescence intensity versus concentration of F−, and a good linear relationship was observed in the range of 0–1.5 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.994. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be about 49 μM based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of blank, s is the slope of the calibration plot). To verify the selectivity of CDs to F− ion, other halide ions such as Cl−, Br− and I− ions, and some cationic ions (e.g., Na+, NH4 +) were also studied. As shown in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence quenching was observed only after the addition of F−, revealing the unique selectivity of CDs for detection of F−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of CDs and its intracellular imaging of F−
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the good optical property of the CDs and its excellent response for F−, we expected the obtained CDs can be employed as a fluorescent probe for cellular imaging. Prior to investigating the cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of CDs against HeLa cells was performed by MTT assay. As displayed in Fig. 6a, the obtained CDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity even the concentration reached 0.8 mg/mL, revealing the good biocompatibility of CDs. To further verify the low toxicity of CDs, the green bean growth experiments were performed. Fig. 6b displays the photography of mung bean sprouts growth in water and CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg/mL) for 5 days. Clearly, no significant difference was observed between them, indicating that CDs have no effect on the growth of green bean sprouts. Moreover, the growth of the green beans in CDs solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp, suggesting the CDs can penetrate into the plant cells and showed excellent biocompatibility. This observation further confirms the low or ignorable biotoxicity of CDs. The unique fluorescence performance and excellent biocompatibility of CDs endow them have promising applications in cell imaging.

Then we investigated the feasibility of using CDs as a fluorescence probe for the detection of F− in biological systems. Fig. 6c displays the confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells cultured with CDs only and CDs in the presence of F− (200 μM). Obviously, intense blue fluorescence emission was observed for the HeLa cells treated with CDs only, while the blue fluorescence signal dramatically decreased in the presence of F−. Moreover, the normal morphology of HeLa cells was not destroyed after incubation with CDs and CDs + F−, suggesting the good biocompatibility of CDs. These above results reveal that the as-prepared CDs can be utilized as fluorescent probe for detection F− in living cells.

In summary, we proposed a green and facile strategy to synthesize the CDs without addition of any additives. The obtained CDs exhibited brightly blue luminescence, excellent water-solubility and good biocompatibility. More importantly, a significant color change can be observed by the naked eye and accompanied by the fluorescence quenching for the CDs upon addition of F− ions, suggesting that the CDs can be employed as a fluorescence and colorimetric dual-model probe for selectively detection of F− ions. Meanwhile, the obtained CDs also can be applied for intracellular sensing of F−.

Shaohua Liu: development of the methodology and writing the initial draft. Zengchen Liu: performing the experiments and data collection. Qingfeng Li: analyze the study data. Hongjun Xia: performing the experiments. Weijie Yang: analyze study data. Ruixia Wang: performing the experiments. Yanxia Li: evidence collection. Hui Zhao: reviewing and editing. Boshi Tian: designed the experimental, reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601213, 21701203 and 21401218), the Key Program of Committee of Education of Henan Province (21A150065) and Innovation Fund of Zhoukou Normal University (ZSHYCX1903).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118964.
